description:
secretx-core/src/lib.rs:275-316: json_parse_string builds the secret value into a plain String via push() in a loop. As the String grows past its initial capacity, it reallocates, freeing the old heap buffer WITHOUT zeroizing it. On a long secret (e.g. a 4 KiB API token) the old 16/32/64/.../2048-byte buffers are all freed back to the allocator un-zeroed, each one containing a complete or partial copy of the secret prefix.

This directly defeats the security guarantee that the SecretValue doc comment (lib.rs:53, lines 19-52) makes the entire reason this hand-rolled scanner exists rather than serde_json. The doc comment says 'only the one requested value is placed into a Zeroizing buffer; everything else is scanned and discarded in place' — but the target value itself is NOT placed into a Zeroizing buffer until parsing completes. During parsing it lives in a non-Zeroizing String whose allocator has been visible to memory pressure for the full duration of the parse.

Trigger: any extract_field / extract_path_field call with a field value larger than ~16 bytes will reallocate at least once. A 1 KiB secret reallocates ~7 times, leaving 7 unzeroed copies of various prefixes scattered in the heap until the allocator reuses each page.

Fix: parse into Zeroizing<String> or Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> — pre-size with String::with_capacity(remaining_input_len) and wrap in Zeroizing before pushing. The same fix applies to scan_string_key_b (lib.rs:689) when the key happens to be sensitive, though keys are typically not secret.

This is the core threat model of this crate. P0.
title: json_parse_string leaks secret bytes via String reallocation
